item.title: I'm loosing my shit over the images
item.content:encoded:
God, these images are so goddamn cool. Like, they are so unique and just straight-up surreal it looks like something out of an actual art piece.

Aside from that though, it was a really good read. Loved it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fantastic, I can even sympathize with the daughters of eden to an extent, but they fucked up, this is great, everything about this is great, classic djkaktus +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Finally read this one after seeing people talk about it so much.
I should have read it sooner. Also props on the editing.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: tfw you join an eco terrorist cult so you can try to date gaia

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Absolutely brilliant. This one is polished damn-near to an edge, and I cannot tell you how much that added to my enjoyment. Excellent images, great use of classification, addenda, bureaucracy, and so forth that just really fleshed it out in a way that I don't know if I can properly articulate. This kind of detailing, that gets into clinical terms, and minutiae, and subtly elaborates on the world of The Foundation is what I LIVE for in these articles. The two big 'reveals' of the article, 'sacrifice everyone' and 'he's sustained by all sacrifices and rituals', stand out as particular strong points.

+ Minor Nitpicks
- Nothing's perfect, but oh do we try

I don't like that "SCP-4971-▽" gets introduced as a term before it's defined. It feels like bad form, and that the first use of it should have a parenthetical along the lines of "SCP-4971-▽ (the primary entity residing within SCP-4971)".

"mostly arboreal" Mostly doesn't feel like the right word. Primarily, maybe?

"Much of the native flora bears a similar appearance to similar species" the double use of similar makes for awkward flow here. Corresponding, analogous, equivalent, comparable?

"The first officers to respond to the scene were unable to locate the trespassers, a large group of people of whom some had been caught attempting to break into the mall at an earlier date." I think this would read much better as "The first officers to respond to the scene were unable to locate the trespassers; a group of [either approximate or specific number] people, a number of whom had been caught attempting to break into the mall at an earlier date."

"Extensive searching of the mall revealed SCP-4971" Perhaps instead "An extensive police [search/investigation] of the mall [uncovered/revealed] the existence of SCP-4971"

"E-13 Nine-Eyes' auditory sensor picks up several unknown sounds, but for some reason the agent does not relay this" The 'for some reason' doesn't work here in the context of this being a written transcript; that particular phrasing is too narrative. "For reason unclear" or "for reasons unknown" perhaps, or even a parenthetical like "picks up several unknown sounds but the agent does not relay this cognitohazard, relay to (whoever or redact it)//" to help sell that it's a transcript being written by Foundation personnel

There's at least two instances where you use "they see" or "they can see" (possibly more that I didn;t catch) in the transcript-of-action portions; these sections should just be a direct transcription of video information for the text log format, not ascribing anything to the agents. These bits should have their language changed to indicate that these things are visible on the video feed, or if such things would not be seen on video, the dialogue should mention that they see it if it's important. Saying that they can see it is tale language, not log language.

Well-deserved +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this literally THE DEER?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, it occurs to me.

If the Foundation decided to stop ALL their 'greater good' sacrifices, dooming uncountable everything in an attempt to neutralize the entity powered by all Sacrifice…

would that action itself constitute a sacrifice and thus sustain the entity anyway?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How would this entity cause a VK-class “end-of-human-habitablity” scenario? Reading it didn’t really give me any hints as to how other then the message at the top.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There were parts that were really appealing, but there were enough problems with the prose and style to keep me largely out of it. When I saw your comment about it being a coldpost, well, that fits.

General issues:

+ show block
– hide block

- Biggest one, there's just a lot of dead text. In the notes describing the book and the mall and the timeline, my regular feeling was "stop telling me about the story and tell me the story." For example, I counted at least three times when the doctor says something like "I won't bore you with the details" but then just says the details. Why even have the middle step?
- (That part wasn't all bad: The description of the Appeal was rather cool and the doctor had decent personality, it could've just used another pass.)
- The timeline of events also included some language that felt like it was trying too hard to sound clinical, and that's not something I've seen in your other writing.
- The first exploration log was a waste of space. Too much direct exposition, agents just saying what happens in a nor-particularly-natural-sounding or interesting manner, not much interesting happening, and it goes on way too long. It made me skip the second log, which is unfortunate because the third log is pretty great.
- The interview was the first time I really got invested, I wish you'd gotten to it sooner.
- The deal with the classification committee… It was a really interesting narrative and pathos, in theory. I enjoy the implications about the Foundation-as-ritual and the choices they had to make, but I don't exactly see why you thought framing that story with the bureaucracy of the classification committee made that story better, or how the "momentous step" of them applying a very specific or new classification was something that would make a reader (ok, me) care. It just seemed tangential to the whole thing.
- The big bad is a really interesting image, but the fact that the head-glyph is perfectly level with the camera makes it look a bit chintzier than it could be.

I dunno, no vote. Cool concepts, just with too much filler.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a very nice article, don't get me wrong. But damn it was long, perhaps too long.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just realized where this is at. US-31 becomes US-69. Good use of stripping details from the map. Great logs, incredible images, love the formatting.

One of my alt time fav scps.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love djkaktus articles, or at least all the ones that I have read. He is capable of creating exploration logs that immerse me in the story, and seems to describe a world that truly exists!

This skip is no exception, and the images just make it better! I loved the surreal shapes, allied with the text description, to give an out of this world feeling…

Some minor complaints: SCP-4971 could be more trimmed. I thought that the initial description, and a lot of details, could be skipped over. Also, the classification, even that I understand the reasoning to change it, could be an uncontained Keter, in my humble opinion…

Last, but not least, SCP-4971-▽ reminded me of another creation from the same author, detailed in the magnificient SCP-1730:

+ SCP-1730 excerpt
- Holy Deer!

"From within the cold, foggy room, a towering, vaguely cervine creature steps out into the main chamber. It is composed of a body covered in light green and cream colored hair, a long, thin neck ending in a hairless, somewhat humanoid face, and vast, intertwined white and black antlers that pulse with streaks of blue light. Floating above its head are nine concentric rings of glowing, rotating crystals and metallic spheres.

The creature slowly steps out of the containment cell and turns to look at the team on the ground below. It opens its mouth and a long, droning sound is heard through the room. Around its body, several large, metallic, cylindrical structures appear followed by a distinct cracking sound. "

So maybe in some other dimension the Foundation discovered a way to contain SCP-4971-▽'s sibling!

item.title: well this was quite something
item.content:encoded: i just finished reading the SCP and i don't know why but i love this format, maybe it just because the story and the field logs are good but i don't know

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The thing is that it is empowered by sacrifice and ritual. All the little things and rituals and superstitions, including the one used to keep BIG SATURN DEER at bay inadvertably power this thing.

Maybe said ritual can power it or try and cheat the wording but even then…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Foundation has killed gods before. Feels like a good way to circumvent the "every human heart" thing is to shish-kebab the deer with the Spear of the Non-Believer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
VOTE TO ESTABLISH CERNNUNOS-CLASS

VOTES FOR

J. Aktus / S. Alexander / C. Ivester / A. Deckard / B. Humphrey / J.R. Sneddon / M. Prince / M. Bridges / J. Sydney

VOTES AGAINST:

T. Paxton / A. Desai

MOTION CARRIES

Cracked me up tbh.

This was really well put together. Definitely one of the better exploration log-heavy skips out there. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm surprised there haven't been more skips in malls, considering their status these days as dying relics of commerce. Also surprised the big Nabla was just another deer. Got me wondering about all kinds of connections to other things, but I expect that's not the point.

I do wish there was a way to code the first footnote so it shows up in the mouse-over a bit wider, and not a thin column that vanishes off the bottom of the screen. Given the use of CSS in this article, I have to imagine that's doable somehow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was an aggressive reaction from Gnath and some of their points don't hold for me, but I really don't think you should paint it as just "aesthetic tastes."

I disagree with the new banner in most cases — I think it's an unwieldy way to start most articles, and doesn't get the user in the right mindset. It answers questions that were already present in the article, that I didn't want answered right away.

People always talk about "format screw" and debate whether or not it's a valuable inclusion. It improves articles for many people. Lessens their impact for many others. Using this new banner has a very real impact on the article as a whole and is more than just a style thing.

Significant graphics/formatting usage is an integral part of article writing on this site, like it or not.

item.title: A lot to process
item.content:encoded:
It took me a few days to get my thoughts straight on this one. Here's what I got:

Pros:

I feel like you really managed to pull off the eco-feminist-witch characters, a series of tropes seems challenging to fit together without crossing a line into 'terribly corny.' Anna and Katarina were both very believable pieces within the narrative, prior to Foundation intervention.

Both exploration logs were tremendous, both in content and pacing. Even until the end, I couldn't find a single fault or misstep in setting the scene inside 4971.

The image manips really helped my ability to engage with the environment, and meshed with the story very effectively.

Cons:

The Internal Memos/Ethics Committee Rulings section was very dense and difficult to work through, which built up the suspense of the anomaly itself for too long. Parts were a bit repetitive, especially regarding definition of the Cernunnos esoteric class and the Ethics Committee's decision to deny effective containment procedures (of course the Ethics Committee wouldn't sacrifice the entire human race to appease a god of rituals), and a large portion of this section could be trimmed down. It could be effective if the memo on the Ethics Committee's ruling is apparently short to inform the reader of how swiftly the Committee was brought to such a unanimous decision, making that conflict between containment and ethics that much more urgent.

The time-shift between the two crews after entering the area didn't really do anything for the story, and I don't think cutting it would affect the interaction between the MTFs at all.

Finally, the Additional Transmissions section at the very end felt disjointed, and without any real focus that added to the narrative. I really expected a final *punch* to be delivered here, but all we got were a few disconnected video and audio feeds from (Mercury?) without a lot of context. This could be where the threat of 4971-V's emergence into central Indiana is realized, perhaps even through some grotesque body horror to ramp up the urgency of the anomaly's threat. Show that he's the god of rituals besides telling, I mean.

For satisfying my desire for spooky exploration logs done right, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Jeez i just saw this with the new class bar and I'm like, blown away at how good it looks? Like, aesthetically this is such a great skip.

One minor thing: one of the images is captioned with: "Image of unknown humanoid entity taken from I-44's video camera." and it should really specify which member of I-44's camera it is since they all have.

item.title: Why the exploration logs?
item.content:encoded:
I like this, but the exploration logs added nothing for me. At this point in the wiki's life, exploration logs need to justify themselves, and this just didn't say much. There was some cool imagery with the mall overtaken by nature, but then they wander out into a wild area. I'm comparing this to, say, the character and plot of 2935, or the imagery and setting of 093, both of which justify exploration logs. The plot and characters of the witches are very interesting, and the encounter with her within the exploration logs was a high point, but most of the plot and character are in the interview. The explorers have no personality, almost intentionally so. The setting outside the mall (how are they keeping these things contained?) and in the nature covered mall is great. However, the log quickly moves to a generic hilly woods.

It also bothers me a little that 2010s is the first the foundation has encountered something that could be contained better, but they choose not to. It seems like there's a lot of things that could be contained better if you had no limits. I just would I expected this issue to come up in the first two decades or so of the Foundation.

item.title: Community Outreach - Junior Staff Post - OPEN
item.content:encoded:
Kaktus, Rounder, pastarasta and ModernMajorGeneral are all right.

Downvoting all the works of an author because you don't like the theme is an incredible disservice to the author, isn't a solution and, in fact, not bring anything. And personally, I find this attitude really despicable.

It's not the first time that you made a comment with extreme overreaction for… nothing. Calm down, man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the parent post is excessively hostile and melodramatic, but I don't agree with this attitude, as I think the perception that readers have no influence on graphics contributes to a good deal of hostility to graphics and UI changes. It's understandable that there is a preference to vote based purely on writing quality, but voting is the only concrete way to influence site content, and it's not surprising that people become resentful if you tell them they can't do that. Voting is not a great system for anything but I think people need to have the ability to express their dissatisfaction with graphics by voting. Anyway, there are plenty of factors other than the skill of the individual author that affect votes and are impossible and undesirable to police (I'm not talking about malicious downvoting here, but something like a perfectly well-written article which gets downvotes for having the same themes as another article would be an example).

(Full disclosure that I downvoted for reasons unrelated to graphics - I thought this was ok and quite enjoyable in parts, but felt the ultimate point of this being some sort of elder sacrifice god was too pedestrian by SCP standards to justify the length or buildup)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i legit understand where all this is coming from, i think we need to chill on the formats too, but this is an incredibly intense emotional response to something that is meaningless in the grand scheme of things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I will downvote all the works that I found containing this theme

wow that seems like an incredible disservice to the author

ignoring the quality of their actual writing and making your judgement of the article based on the author's aesthetic tastes rather than their skill at writing

does not sit right

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow there's uh… a lot to unpack here.

Nothing I can say is going to make you happy, I think, so suffice to say that I won't stop using this format, and I very much doubt that anyone is going to force me to stop using it. I like it. I wouldn't have used it if I didn't like it lmao.

I'm sorry you don't, but it's my article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Show comment
- Hide comment

I've read, three days ago, this scip, that a friend of mine proposed to me. Today, I log, I learn the new theme that just came out, and I panically cross all the pages were this new theme could appear. I really liked the Cernunnos, even with the Nu-theme, but there, it just feel… wrong. Not an SCP. Not even an SCP related format. And what make me even sadder is that you have already the means to signify what you want to express by this. Creating subclasses "à la CWW". Creating your own subclasses system, without a new theme. Or reusing the color indicator who is used in some Int classes, who work well without breaking the suspension of disbelief but that you didn't like for reasons. I want a good SCP, but also an SCP that I could read without breaking immediately my suspension of disbelief or the one of my friends who reads SCP and who ask me for the scips that I found the better. I don't like downvote only for the format. I don't like the Nu theme, but I support it when the scip is good or justify its use. However, this is just a massive anachrony on the scip, a format so huge that some Serie I SCPs are less imposant on the page, even 173, and whose "emojis" are, at the best, average. For now, I'm not happy to do it, but I will downvote all the works that I found containing this theme until somebody chose to force the normal SCP standard form on all the new themes when it's unneeded. I'm a reader, my voice is certainly less important that all the great authors there on this site, but I beg all the good writers who want to use this theme without it being needed to not do it. Please. If everybody come with his own theme, the site is doomed. Use the Nu-theme, use the new classes without this format; but please, stop using this format. It just… ruins everything for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good stuff.

This made feel things, mainly tension and curiosity, but also some chuckles, and a few oh craps. Any skip that makes me feel things is doing something right.

The standout bits for me were "Good news, there is an upper limit. The upper limit is everyone."; "He is the god of sacrifice."; and "yeah sure you can kill it, just stop doing any and all rituals, including all the containment procedures.".

Foundation are probably unwittingly one of the most devoted worshippers of this skip. They may not actively consciously worship it, but they do give it power.

Oh, yeah, and all the neat media work, containment class shenanigans, Three Portlands name-drop, all the little stuff, is all just the gravy on top. Not something I would have upvoted just for, but I appreciate it on top of the otherwise solid article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This gave me chills! Great work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
He's not?!!
Oh man, I gotta cancel my appointment…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hot damn, this is some good stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is what bugged me as well. I think you're right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the images. They do a great job at making what would otherwise be silly imagery into actually something creepy and compelling. Plus they are just straight up good photos.

My only major negative with the article is the initial presentation of -▽. At this point of the wiki's life, end of the world, Capital Letter, eldritch abominations are a dime a dozen, and don't really have much impact when there's fifty other similar entities being dealt with. The occultism aspects do a bit to relieve some of it, and the final reveal of -▽ does make it much more interesting. I just think the beginning is somewhat weak compared to the rest of the article.

That aside, easy +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good horror leaves a lingering impact that can last for hours.
Great horror will leave an impact that can last for days, weeks, months, or even years.
10 days have passed before I read this masterpiece and I wouldn't be surprised if the impact lasted longer than that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Don't let the One Who Knows Silence perform your heart surgery.

He's not a real doctor.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, well. You can't win em all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not gonna call the object class a dealbreaker, but I do feel like it's a stretch. The rest was pleasant to read, but that class is the one thing keeping me from +1ing…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not quite. The growth defined is exponential (continously doubling). Logarithmic, however, starts with a high number and diminishes.

Logistic growth is what you're looking for. Starts slow, speeds up, levels out. That's the way populations grow, as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I’m not as big of a fan of this as everyone else. The first few addenda feel like justification for an object class that could’ve easily been Keter. In addition, the ending felt anticlimactic to me, although I feel like I’m missing something.

Otherwise, I like this. The exploration logs are definitely classic as always, and the overall concept is fresh. Also, this is the first skip that I know of to feature an abandoned mall, which are definitely cursed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A. Desei is a reference to Arvind Desai from the tale I Thought You Died Alone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like the nod to Cimmerian. Are any of the other Ethics peeps someone I should recognize?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, logarithmically is correct because there is an upper limit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: fuck

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you cannot imagine the creatures in the pictures moving about and interacting with things I would say that that is a simple perceptual difference between yourself and most people. I don't know that there's a more granular explanation, it just is what it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't have much to say here, but yeah this is how you sell something as warranting an eso-class.

Also the pictures are really cool. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
and it scales logarithmically

Shouldn't this be exponentially? Logarithmic means slowing down.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For me, the fact that the images look fake makes them feel real. These entities are meant to be otherworldly and unnatural, things that any person would struggle to conceptualize. The fact that they just look like triangles photoshopped onto the images works in their favor because nothing real should look like that. And yet, these things do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great descriptions, excellent dialogue, and very strong pacing. Everything I've come to expect from your work, djkaktus. The edited images adds a layer of polish that ties the whole piece together really well. The ending hits me the hardest, for sure. I've always had a weakness for articles like 1958, where the closing note is that the story continues on for weeks and months. The article builds up the readers imagination, then the closing time-jump gives them a space to put those thoughts. Well done. +1

item.title: Re: But each day, as she walks to the sea
item.content:encoded: Noting that the images have been sourced under the info tab.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dead Mall Series: This Time It's Nature Cthulhu

someone get dan bell on the phone for a really good exploration log. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Absolutely loved this scip. Had the perfect forest-y atmosphere and the pictures helped execute it really well too. The descriptor of the god in this scenario also made it more interesting. I love the concept of an entity sustained by rituals in general, because it kind of means that everyone in the Foundation's universe is sort of at fault, whether it be the Mekhanites or Sarkics and the Foundation itself.

One thing that intrigued me was the E-13's mention of something happening in '84. Which reference is that to?

Easy +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would say that's not entirely true. Differences in perception can be identified and quantified, and I know for a fact that there are systemic differences between my perception and that of an average human (I have high-functioning autism), but I don't know exactly what those differences are.
However, it is possible to identify and characterize those differences by using the inconsistencies between my perception, excepted neurotipical perception (built from a model) and real neurotipical perception (inferred from observation if available).
I'm interested in the answer precisely because of the large difference between the expected perception and the real perception, which seems to indicate a a large flaw in the model, most likely a fundamental perception difference I haven't figured out yet -hence why I'm trying to collect data about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're asking to have someone explain to you why they can suspend their disbelief where you can't. There isn't an answer to that; it's a personal experience based on your own perception and how the article takes you there.

If you remain above the surface of the water, everything beneath it will always appear distorted relative to those within.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this was absolutely bonkers and i really loved it, i really like that you chose for this to center around a mall. the visuals (not talking about the pictures themselves, just talking about what goes on, the pictures ARE really good tho imo) are really cool and freaky, and the exploration logs were super fun. great job on this!

1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, I know about the general concept of simplicity in design, I guess I didn't express my thoughts properly. When I talked about the design being "simplistic" and the sharpie on a photograph analogy, the idea behind it is that all I can see when looking at those image is the computer behind them, and I really can't bring my mind to see them as anything other than a triangle photoshoped into an image rather than the anomalous camera images suspension of disbelief would want me to see them as. Maybe this the key to why I conceptualized them wrong?
And I do guess my metrics place too much importance on difficulty of creation, in hindsight.
Still feel like I'm leaving with more questions than answers but thank you very much for trying to answer my interrogations!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What I mean is, a design is not made great because of its complexity. A design is made great because of the way it is applied by the designer. Effective use of simplicity has been a staple of design themes for decades. A lot of people will like a thing more because of their emotional response to it rather than their impression of how complex it is or how difficult it was to create.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it's best to reiterate that I really don't have any ill will with that comment about the images, and want in no way to insult and diminish the work that has been done on either the images or the article.
My main motivation here truly curiosity and the will to understand the dissonance between reality and my predictive model of what it would be; because I currently fail to find the flaw that would create such a large prediction error.

I think it's probably fair to say that sometimes, in design, it's less about what you do and more about how you do it.

Would you care to elaborate what you mean in this particular context by this? Not being about what you do here would mean "it's not really about the actually design of the image" but fail to understand what "how you do it" would refer to, since method of creation would probably be unknown to the viewer. Do you mean execution as referring to the context the image exist into (here the article) or something else?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno man, maybe people just thought they were cool. There's obviously more going on there than the line tool, but I think it's probably fair to say that sometimes, in design, it's less about what you do and more about how you do it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, I'm really curious about details on people's opinion of the images in that article. The comments here are extremely positive toward those images and that's a big dissonance with the reaction I would have expected and I'm very curious about what kind of error in my model is causing this difference.
To elaborate on why I didn't expect a positive reaction to those images (and I really don't mean any disrespect nor bad will toward the author of those pictures here) is because they are extremely simplistic (just geometric shapes with a glow) and feel like the photoshop equivalent of drawing in sharpie over a photograph, and imo kinda break the immersion because of that.
And because the popular reaction to these image seems to be very opposite to that sentiment, I am very curious about why people like those images as they are, and would really love an explanation by people who really liked them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is brilliant
